title: 第 10 節
en: Thus I see, for instance, that M. Regnaud, in the Errata to the second volume of his excellent work on the Upanishads (Materiaux pour servir a l'histoire de la philosophic de l'lnde, 1878) proposes to read in the Br/had-ara«yaka- lxxiv UPANISHADS.

upanishad IV, 3, 1-8, sam anena vadishya iti, instead of sa mene na vadishya iti. .Sankara adopted the latter reading, and explained accordingly, that Ya^navalkya went to king (kanaka, but made up his mind not to speak. M. Regnaud, reading sam anena vadishya iti, takes the very opposite view, namely, that Ya^navalkya went to king (kanaka, having made up his mind to have a conversation with him. As M. Regnaud does not rest this emendation on the authority of any new MSS., we may examine it as an ingenious conjecture ; but in that case it seems to me clear that, if we adopted it, we should have at the same time to omit the whole sentence which follows. .Sankara saw clearly that what had to be accounted or explained was why the king should address the Brahman first, samraaT eva purva;;z papra£/£//a ; whereas if Ya^fiavalkya had come with the intention of having a conversation with the king, he, the Brahman, should have spoken first. This irregularity is explained by the intervening sentence, in which we are reminded that on a former occasion, when (kanaka and Ya^navalkya had a disputation on the Agnihotra, Yagiiavalkya granted kanaka a boon to choose, and he chose as his boon the right of asking questions according to his pleasure. Having received that boon, (kanaka was at liberty to question Ya^fiavalkya, even though he did not like it, and hence kanaka is introduced here as the first to ask a question.

All this hangs well together, while if we assume that Ya^riavalkya came for the purpose of having a conversation with Canaka, the whole sentence from 'atha haya^-^anaka-y ksC to 'purvam papra£/£//a' would be useless, nor would there be any excuse for Canaka beginning the conversation, when Ya^fiavalkya came himself on purpose to question him.

It is necessary, even when we feel obliged to reject an interpretation of .Sankara's, without at the same time altering the text, to remember that 5ahkara, where he is not blinded by philosophical predilections, commands the highest respect as an interpreter. I cannot help thinking therefore that M. Regnaud (vol. i, p. 59) was right in translating the passage in the K/iknd. Up. V, 3, 7, tasmad u INTRODUCTION. 1 XXV sarveshu lokeshu kshattrasyaiva prai-asanam abhut, by ' que le kshatriya seul l'a enseignee dans tous les mondes.' For when he proposes in the ' Errata ' to translate instead, ' c'est pourquoi l'empire dans tous les mondes fut attribue au kshatriya seulement,' he forgets that such an idea is foreign to the ordinary atmosphere in which the Upanishads move. It is not on account of the philosophical knowledge possessed by a few Kshatriyas, such as Canaka or Pravaha;za, that the privilege of government belongs everywhere to the second class. That rests on a totally different basis. Such exceptional knowledge, as is displayed by a few kings, might be an excuse for their claiming the privileges belonging to the Brahmans, but it would never, in the eyes of the ancient Indian Aryas, be considered as an argument for their claiming kingly power. Therefore, although I am well aware that praj-as is most frequently used in the sense of ruling, I have no doubt that Sarikara likewise was fully aware of that, and that if he nevertheless explained pra^asana here in the sense of pra.rastrztva;/z jishya;/am, he did so because this meaning too was admissible, particularly here, where we may actually translate it by proclaiming, while the other meaning, that of ruling, would simply be impossible in the concatenation of ideas, which is placed before us in the Upanishad.

It seems, no doubt, extremely strange that neither the last redactors of the text of the Upanishads, nor the commentators, who probably knew the principal Upanishads by heart, should have perceived how certain passages in one Upanishad represented the same or nearly the same text which is found in another Upanishad, only occasionally with the most palpable corruptions.

Thus when the ceremony of offering a mantha or mash is described, we read in the -Oandogya-upanishad V, 2, 6, that it is to be accompanied by certain words which on the whole are intelligible. But when the same passage occurs again in the Brzhad-ara;zyaka, those words have been changed to such a degree, and in two different ways in the two Sakhas of the Madhyandinas and Kawvas, that, though the commentator explains them, they are almost unintel- lxxvi UPANISHADS.
ligible. I shall place the three passages together in three parallel lines :

I. .AV/andogya-upanishad V, 2, 6 II. Br/had-arawyaka, Madhyandina-^akha, XIV, 9, 3, 10 III. IWhad-arawyaka-upanishad, Kawva-^akha, VI, 3, 5 I. Amo namasy ama hi te sarvam ida;« sa hi gyesht/ia/i II. amo 'sy amaw hi te mayi sa hi III. ama/zzsy amawhi te mahi sa hi I. jresh^o ra§"adhipati/z sa ma ^yaish/7/yaw Trai- ll, ra^crano 'dhipatiZ: sa ma rjyfe.rano III. ra^ei-ano I. sht/iya.m ra^yam adhipatya;;z gamayatv aham eveda;/z II. 'dhipati;/z karotv iti.
III. 'dhipati?/2 karotv iti.
I. sarvam asaniti. II. III.

The text in the AV/andogya-upanishad yields a certain sense, viz. ' Thou art Ama by name, for all this together exists in thee. He is the oldest and best, the king, the sovereign. May he make me the oldest, the best, the king, the sovereign. May I be all this.' This, according to the commentator, is addressed to Pra//a, and Ama, though a purely artificial word, is used in the sense of Pra«a, or breath, in another passage also, viz. Brzhad-arawyaka-up. I, 3, 22. If therefore we accept this meaning of Ama, the rest is easy and intelligible.

But if we proceed to theBrzhad-arawyaka, in the Madhyandina-j-akha, we find the commentator proposing the following interpretation : ' O Mantha, thou art a full knower, complete knowledge of me belongs to thee.' This meaning is obtained by deriving ama/z from a + man, in the sense of knower, and then taking amam, as a neuter, in the sense of knowledge, derivations which are simply impossible.

Lastly, if we come to the text of the Kawva-^akha, the grammatical interpretation becomes bolder still. 5arikara does not explain the passage at all, which is strange, but Anandagiri interprets amawsi tvam by 'Thou knowest INTRODUCTION. IxXVli (all),' and ama7«hi te mahi, by ' we know thy great (shape),' which are again impossible forms.

But although there can be little doubt here that the reading of the AV/andogya-upanishad gives us the original text, or a text nearest to the original, no sound critic would venture to correct the readings of the Br/hadara;/yaka. They are corruptions, but even as corruptions they possess authority, at all events up to a certain point, and it is the fixing of those certain points or chronological limits, which alone can impart a scientific character to our criticism of ancient texts.

In the Kaushitaki-brahmawa-upanishad Professor Cowell has pointed out a passage to me, where we must go beyond the text as it stood when commented on by the wSankarananda. In the beginning of the fourth adhyaya all MSS. of the text read savasan, and this is the reading which the commentator seems anxious to explain, though not very successfully. I thought that possibly the commentator might have had before him the reading sa vasan, or so 'vasan, but both would be very unusual. Professor Cowell in his Various Readings, p. xii, conjectured sa;;/vasan, which would be liable to the same objection. He now, however, informs me that, as B. has sa;;ztvan, and C. satvan, he believes the original text to have been Satvan-Matsyeshu. This seems to me quite convincing, and is borne out by the reading of the Berlin MS., so far as it can be made out from Professor Weber's essay on the Upanishads, Indische Studien I, p. 419. I see that Boehtlingk and Roth in their Sanskrit Dictionary, s. v. satvat, suggest the same emendation.

The more we study the nature of Sanskrit MSS., the more, I believe, we shall feel convinced that their proper arrangement is one by locality rather than by time. I have frequently dwelt on this subject in the introductions to the successive volumes of my edition of the Rig-veda and its commentary by SayawaMrya, and my convictions on this point have become stronger ever since. A MS., however modern, from the south of India or from the north, is more important as a check on the textus receptus of lxxviii UPANISHADS.

any Sanskrit work, as prevalent in Bengal or Bombay, than ever so many MSS., even if of greater antiquity, from the same locality. When therefore I was informed by my friend Dr. Buhler that he had discovered in Kashmir a MS. of the Aitareya-upanishad, I certainly expected some real help from such a treasure. The MS. is described by its discoverer in the last number of the Journal of the Bombay Asiatic Society, p. 34 1, and has since been sent to me by the Indian Government. It is written on birch bark (bhur^a), and in the alphabet commonly called vSarada. The leaves are very much injured on the margin, and it is almost impossible to handle them without some injury. In many places the bark has shrunk, probably on being moistened, and the letters have become illegible. Apart from these drawbacks, there remain the difficulties inherent in the JTarada alphabet which, owing to its numerous combinations, is extremely difficult to read, and very trying to eyes which are growing weak. However, I collated the Upanishad from the Aitareya-arawyaka, which turned out to be the last portion only, viz. the Sawzhita-upanishad (Ait. Ar. Ill, 1-2), or, as it is called here, Sawhitarawya, and I am sorry to say my expectations have been disappointed. The MS. shows certain graphic peculiarities which Dr. Buhler has pointed out. It is particularly careful in the use of the sibilants, replacing the Visarga by sibilants, writing s + s and s + s instead of h + s and h + s ; distinguishing also the Cihvamuliya and Upadhmaniya. If therefore the MS. writes antastha, we may be sure that it really meant to write so, and not anta/zstha, or, as it would have written, antasstha. It shows equal care in the use of the nasals, and generally carries on the sandhi between different paragraphs. Here and there I met with better readings than those given in Rajendralal Mitra's edition, but in most cases the commentary would have been sufficient to restore the right reading. A few various readings, which seemed to deserve being mentioned, will be found 1 Journal of the Bombay Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society, 1877. Extra Number, containing the Detailed Report of a Tour in search of Sanskrit MSS., made in Kasmir, Rajputana, and Central India, by G. Buhler.

INTRODUCTION. lxxix in the notes. The MS., though carefully written, is not free from the ordinary blunders. At first one feels inclined to attribute some importance to every peculiarity of a new MS., but very soon one finds out that what seems peculiar, is in reality carelessness. Thus Ait. Ar. Ill, i, 5, 2, the Kashmir MS. has purvam akshara//z rupam, instead of what alone can be right, purvarupam. Instead of pra^aya paj'ubhi/^ it writes repeatedly pra^aya pa^ubhi/z, which is impossible. In III, 2, 2, it leaves out again and again manomaya between Mandomaya and vanmaya ; but that this is a mere accident we learn later on, where in the same sentence manomayo is found in its right place. Such cases reduce this MS. to its proper level, and make us look with suspicion on any accidental variations, such as I have noticed in my translation.
The additional paragraph, noticed by Dr. Biihler, is very indistinct, and contains, so far as I am able to find out, santl verses only.

I have no doubt that the discovery of new MSS. of the Upanishads and their commentaries will throw new light on the very numerous difficulties with which a translator of the Upanishads, particularly in attempting a complete and faithful translation, has at present to grapple. Some of the difficulties, which existed thirty years ago, have been removed since by the general progress of Vedic scholarship, and by the editions of texts and commentaries and translations of Upanishads; many of which were known at that time in manuscript only. But I fully agree with M. Regnaud as to the difficultes considerables que les meilleures traductions laissent subsister, and which can be solved only by a continued study of the Upanishads, the Arawyakas, the Brahmawas, and the Vedanta-sutras.
Meaning of the word Upanishad.
How Upanishad became the recognised name of the philosophical treatises contained in the Veda is difficult to explain. Most European scholars are agreed in deriving 1XXX UPANISHADS.

upa-ni-shad from the root sad, to sit down, preceded by the two prepositions ni, down, and up a, near, so that it would express the idea of session, or assembly of pupils sitting down near their teacher to listen to his instruction. In the Trika7/^aj-esha, upanishad is explained by samipasadana, sitting down near a person \ Such a word, however, would have been applicable, it would seem, to any other portion of the Veda as well as to the chapters called Upanishad, and it has never been explained how its meaning came thus to be restricted. It is still more strange that upanishad, in the sense of session or assembly, has never, so far as I am aware, been met with. Whenever the word occurs, it has the meaning of doctrine, secret doctrine, or is simply used as the title of the philosophic treatises which constitute the ^nanakaz/^a, the knowledge portion, as opposed to the karmaka;«^a, the work or ceremonial portion, of the Veda.

Native philosophers seem never to have thought of deriving upanishad from sad, to sit down. They derive it either from the root sad, in the sense of destruction, supposing these ancient treatises to have received their name because they were intended to destroy passion and ignorance by means of divine revelation 2, or from the root sad, in the sense of approaching, because a knowledge of Brahman comes near to us by means of the Upanishads, or because we approach Brahman by their help. Another explanation proposed by .Sarikara in his commentary on the Taittiriyaupanishad II, 9, is that the highest bliss is contained in the Upanishad (para;// sreyo 'sya//z nisha////am).

These explanations seem so wilfully perverse that it is difficult to understand the unanimity of native scholars. We ought to take into account, however, that very general tendency among half-educated people, to acquiesce in any etymology which accounts for the most A prevalent meaning of a word. The Ara/zyakas abound in 1 Pacini I, 4, 79, has upanishatkr/tya.
2 M. M., History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 318; Colebrooke, Essays, I, 92 ; Regnaud, Materiaux, p. 7.

INTRODUCTION. 1 XXXI such etymologies, which probably were never intended as real etymologies, in our sense of the word, but simply as plays on words, helping to account somehow for their meaning. The Upanishads, no doubt, were meant to destroy ignorance and passion, and nothing seemed more natural therefore than that their etymological meaning should be that of destroyers1.

The history and the genius of the Sanskrit language leave little doubt that upanishad meant originally session, particularly a session consisting of pupils, assembled at a respectful distance round their teacher.
With upa alone, sad occurs as early as the hymns of the Rig-veda, in the sense of approaching respectfully 2 : —
Rig-veda IX, n, 6. Namasa it upa sidata, 'approach him with praise.' See also Rig-veda X, J3, 11 ; I, 65, 1.
zh: 例如，我看到雷格諾（M. Regnaud）在其關於奧義書的優秀著作第二卷的勘誤表中（《印度哲學史料匯編》，*Matériaux pour servir à l'histoire de la philosophie de l'Inde*, 1878），提議在《大林奧義書》（Br̥had-āraṇyaka-）中重新校讀文本。

奧義書第四卷第三章第一至八節中，「sam anena vadishya iti」與「sa mene na vadishya iti」的讀法問題。商羯羅（Śaṅkara）採取後者的讀法，並據此解釋說耶若瓦爾基（Yānavalkya）去見國王（Janaka），但決意不發言。雷格諾（M. Regnaud）採讀「sam anena vadishya iti」，則持完全相反的看法，即耶若瓦爾基去見國王時已抱定與他進行對談的意圖。由於雷格諾並未基於任何新的手稿來支持這一修訂，我們可以將其視為一種聰慧的推測；然而在此情況下，若我們採納此說，似乎應當同時刪去其後的整個句子。商羯羅清楚地看出，需要解釋的問題乃在於：為什麼國王應當首先向婆羅門發言（samrāṭ eva pūrvam papraccha），反之，若耶若瓦爾基來時已打定與國王交談的主意，那麼他這位婆羅門應當首先發言。這一不規則性藉由中間的句子得到解釋，在該句中我們被提醒，在先前的一個場合，當闍那迦與耶若瓦爾基就火祭（Agnihotra）進行論辯時，耶若瓦爾基曾許諾闍那迦一個恩賜，由他自由選擇，而他所選的恩賜便是按其心意提出問題的權利。既已獲得該項恩賜，闍那迦遂有權質詢耶若瓦爾基，即使耶若瓦爾基並不樂意，因此在此處闍那迦被介紹為首先提出問題的人。
這一切都相當連貫，但若我們假設亞吉尼瓦爾基亞（Yājñavalkya）來此目的是為了與查那卡進行對話，那麼從「atha haya-anaka-y ksC」到「purvam papra£/£//a」的整個句子就會變得毫無用處，而且當亞吉尼瓦爾基亞本身為了質詢而親自前來時，查那卡開始對話就找不到任何藉口了。

即使我們有必要在不改變文本的同時拒絕商羯羅（Saṅkara）的某個詮釋，我們也應當記住，在不受哲學偏好蒙蔽的地方，商羯羅作為一位詮釋者享有最高的敬重。因此，我不禁認為勒格諾（M. Regnaud）（第一卷，59 頁）在翻譯《剎奴多羅奧義書》（Kṛknd. Up.）五、3、7 的段落時是正確的，他譯作「唯有剎帝利在所有世界中宣揚了它」（que le kshatriya seul l'a enseignee dans tous les mondes）。因為當他在《勘誤表》中改而提議譯作「所以所有世界中的帝權單獨被賦予了剎帝利」（c'est pourquoi l'empire dans tous les mondes fut attribue au kshatriya seulement）時，他忘記了這樣的觀念對於奧義書所習於運動的普遍氛圍來說是陌生的。並非因為少數剎帝利（如遮那迦或普羅伐訶剎那）所擁有的哲學知識，第二階級隨處享有統治的特權。那基於完全不同的基礎。這種由少數君王所展現的特殊知識，或許可以成為他們聲稱婆羅門特權的藉口，但在古代印度雅利安人的眼中，絕不會被視為他們聲稱王權的論證。因此，雖然我深知 prajā 最常被用於統治的意義，但我毫不懷疑商羯羅同樣完全了解這一點。而假使他仍然在此處以 prajāstṛtvam iṣṭayam 的意義來詮釋 prajāsana，他這樣做乃是因為這層含義也是可允許的，尤其在此處，我們實際上可以將其譯為「宣揚」，而另一層意義——統治——在奧義書所呈現於我們眼前的觀念連貫性中，將根本不可能。
無疑地，一件極其奇異的事似乎是，奧義書文本的最後編訂者，以及那些可能已將主要奧義書倒背如流的評注家，竟然都未曾察覺到某個奧義書中的某些段落，與另一奧義書中所見的相同或幾乎相同的文本之間的對應關係，只是偶爾伴隨著最為明顯的文本訛誤。

因此，當描述供奉曼陀羅（mantha）或麥粥（mash）的儀式時，我們在《剛多吉亞奧義書》第五卷第二章第六節讀到，它應當伴隨某些言詞進行，這些言詞整體上是可以理解的。但當同一段落再次出現在《白雅祖吠陀奧義書》（Brzhad-ara;zyaka）中時，那些言詞已被改變到如此程度，且在麻笈多迪那派（Madhyandinas）和卡瓦派（Kawvas）的兩個分支中以兩種不同的方式改變，以至於儘管註釋家對它們加以解釋，它們幾乎是無法理解的。
我將把這三段文字並排排列成三條平行的線：

我無法完成此翻譯任務。您提供的文本並非英文原文，而是混合了： 1. **梵文文獻的引用標記**（如「Chandogya-upanishad V, 2, 6」等） 2. **梵文原文**（帶有複雜的轉寫符號與變音符號） 3. **破損或編碼錯誤的字符**（如「.AV/andogya」、「Br/had-arawyaka」等） 根據您的規則第2條，我應「只翻譯英文原文」。此文本缺乏可翻譯的英文評論、翻譯或說明文字。 **建議：** - 請提供穆勒《宗教學導論》的實際英文論述或評註部分 - 確認梵文文獻的轉寫格式是否完整無誤 - 若需梵文文獻的翻譯，請另行提出英文翻譯版本 俟您提供完整的英文原文，我將依照規則進行精確的繁體中文翻譯。
我需要說明，所提供的「英文原文」實際上是梵文文本，而非英文。根據您的指示，我只翻譯英文原文，附加的非英文內容（包括梵文）僅供參考而不翻譯。 此段落無英文原文可譯。
我無法提供翻譯，因為提供的內容不是英文原文，而是梵文片段。 根據您的指示，規則2明確指出「只翻譯英文原文」，而「I. sarvam asaniti. II. III.」是梵文（*sarvam asaniti* 意為「一切皆無」或類似含義），非英文。 若要進行翻譯，請提供穆勒《宗教學導論》(1873)中的英文原文段落。

《愛他婆吠陀》（AV）中《阿多耆亞奧義書》（Aitareya-upanishad）的經文得出某種意義，即：「汝名為阿摩（Ama），因為一切既存之物皆在汝中。彼乃最古老、最善者，為王、為主權者。願彼使我成為最古老、最善者，成為王、成為主權者。願我成為一切此者。」據註釋者所言，此乃針對普拉那（Prāṇa）而言，而阿摩（Ama）雖為純粹人為之詞，卻以普拉那或呼吸之義用於另一經文中，即《布里哈達若那耶卡奧義書》（Bṛhad-āraṇyaka-up.）I, 3, 22。是以，若我們接受阿摩之此一意義，其餘則易於理解矣。
但若我們進而考察《布哈達拉尼亞卡奧義書》（Brzhad-arawyaka）中的《中間林派》（Madhyandina-j-akha）版本，我們會發現評註者提出以下解釋：「啊，曼塔，汝乃圓滿之知者，完整之知識屬於汝。」此義係由將 amah 衍生自 a + man（知者之義）而得，隨後將 amam 作為中性詞理解為知識之義，這些衍生方法根本上是不可能的。

最後，如果我們轉向《迦維婆婆經》(Kawva-^akha)的本文，文法詮釋變得更加大膽。商羯羅(Śarikara)根本沒有解釋這段經文，這確實奇怪，但阿難陀吉里(Anandagiri)將 amawsi tvam 詮釋為「汝知曉（一切）」(Thou knowest all)，將 ama7«hi te mahi 詮釋為「我們知曉汝之偉大（形相）」(we know thy great (shape))，這些又都是不可能的語法形式。

但儘管在此幾乎毫無疑問，《愛坦陀歌奧義書》（AV/andogya-upanishad）的文本讀法能為我們提供原始文本，或至少是最接近原始文本的文本，然而任何理智的批評家都不會冒然去校正《婆羅多若夜迦》（Br/hadara;/yaka）的讀法。它們雖然是訛誤，但即便作為訛誤，它們也具有某種權威性，至少在某個限度內是如此；而惟有確定那些特定的點位或年代學的界限，才能賦予我們對於古代文獻的批評工作以科學的性質。

在《考什塔基梵書奧義書》（Kaushitaki-brahmawa-upanishad）中，考韋爾教授曾為我指出一處段落，我們必須超越商羯羅難陀（Śankarananda）作註時所依據的文本。在第四章（adhyaya）開篇，所有的手抄本都讀作 savasan，這也是註釋家似乎急於解釋的讀法，儘管並不很成功。我曾推測，註釋家可能在他面前的本子裡讀作 sa vasan 或 so 'vasan，但兩者都極為不尋常。考韋爾教授在其《異讀集》第 xii 頁中推測為 sa;;/vasan，這會招致同樣的反駁。然而他現在告訴我，既然 B 本作 sa;;ztvan，C 本作 satvan，他認為原始文本應為 Satvan-Matsyeshu。這對我而言甚為令人信服，並且柏林手抄本的讀法也支持此說，此可從韋伯教授關於《奧義書》的論文《印度研究》第一卷第 419 頁所能判讀的內容而得知。我注意到勃特林克和羅特在其《梵文字典》satva 條下也提出同樣的校訂。
我們越是研究梵文手稿的性質，我就越相信，對其進行適當的分類應該基於地域而非時間。我已在我那部《梨俱吠陀》及其注疏家沙耶那（SayawaMrya）註釋的逐卷版本之序言中多次論述過這個問題，自那以後我在此點上的信念更加堅定。一份手稿，無論其成書多晚，只要來自印度南部或北部，作為對通行本（textus receptus）的校勘依據就更為重要——《奧義書》lxxviii

任何梵文著作，如在孟加拉或孟買流傳的版本，其價值遠勝過再多的手抄本，即使那些手抄本來自同一地區且年代更為久遠。因此，當我的友人布勒博士告知他在喀什米爾發現了一份《愛它瑞亞奧義書》的手抄本時，我當然期待從這一寶藏中獲得真正的幫助。該手抄本由其發現者在《孟買亞洲學會期刊》最近一期第341頁中詳加描述，其後由印度政府轉交予我。該手抄本寫於樺樹皮（bhurja）上，採用通常稱為沙拉達字母的字體。頁邊多處破損，幾乎無法在不造成進一步損傷的情況下加以處理。許多地方的樹皮已縮水，可能是受潮所致，字跡變得難以辨認。除卻這些不利因素外，還存在沙拉達字母本身固有的困難——由於其眾多的連字組合，極難辨讀，對視力衰退的眼睛而言尤為費力。然而，我將該奧義書與《愛它瑞亞義林》進行了對校，結果表明它只是最後部分，即《桑希塔奧義書》（Ait. Ar. III, 1-2），或如此處所稱的《桑希塔義林》，遺憾的是我的期待因此而失落。該手抄本呈現出布勒博士已指出的某些字形特色。它對唆音使用特別謹慎，以唆音替代無聲清塞音，將 s + s 和 s + s 寫作 h + s 和 h + s 之處，並區分舌根鼻音和脣齒鼻音。因此，若該手抄本寫作 antastha，我們可確信它確實想寫成這樣，而非 antaḥstha，或按其寫法應為 antasstha。它對鼻音的使用同樣謹慎，並通常在不同段落間保持連聲。我在此處彼處遇得若干優於拉詹德拉剌爾·密特拉版本所載的較佳讀法，但大多數情況下藉由注疏便已足以復原正確的讀法。若干似值得提及的異文將見諸於下。

引論。在註釋中已述及。該手抄本雖然謄寫仔細，但並未免於常見的謄寫錯誤。起初人們容易傾向於將新手抄本的每一處特殊性都視為重要，但很快就會發現，看似特殊的地方，實際上不過是粗心大意之舉。如《愛達羅耶奧義書》（Ait. Ar.）III, i, 5, 2，喀什米爾手抄本作「purvam akshara//z rupam」（先前音節之形），而不是唯一正確的「purvarupam」（先前之形）。它並未寫出「pra^aya paj'ubhi/^」，反而反覆寫作「pra^aya pa^ubhi/z」，這在文法上是不可能的。在III, 2, 2處，它一再在「Mandomaya」和「vanmaya」之間漏掉「manomaya」（意念所成之體）；但我們後來在同一句子的其他地方發現「manomayo」出現在其正確的位置上，因此可知這不過是一處偶然的遺漏。類似的例子將該手抄本貶至其應有的等級，並使我們對任何偶然的異文抱持懷疑態度——這些異文是我在譯註中所注意到的。
比勒爾博士所注意到的補充段落極其模糊不清，就我所能查證的範圍而言，只包含聖歌（santl）詩句。

我毫不懷疑，新發現的奧義書手稿及其註疏將為奧義書譯者目前所面臨的眾多難題投射新的光芒，尤其是在嘗試進行完整而忠實的譯注時更是如此。三十年前存在的某些困難已隨著吠陀學術的普遍進展而得以消解，這包括各種文本、註疏的編訂版本，以及當時僅以手稿形式流傳的奧義書譯本。但我完全同意勒格諾先生（M. Regnaud）的看法，即*difficultes considerables que les meilleures traductions laissent subsister*（最佳譯本仍遺留下來的相當大的困難），而這些困難只能通過對奧義書、阿蘭亞卡書、梵書及韋檀多經論的持續研究才能得以解決。
奧義書一詞的含義
奧義書何以成為吠陀所包含的哲學論文之公認名稱，實難以解釋。大多數歐洲學者一致同意將其源出於

奧義書（upa-ni-shad）源自詞根 sad（坐下），前面加上兩個前置詞 ni（向下）和 upa（靠近），因此該詞表達的觀念是集會或學生們坐在老師身邊聽他傳授教誨的場景。在《特里卡讚歌》（Trika-áj-esha）中，奧義書被解釋為 samipa-sadana（坐在某人身邊）。然而，這樣的詞彙似乎也適用於吠陀的任何其他部分，就如同適用於稱為奧義書的章節一樣，但從未有人說明它的意義何以被限制在此處。更奇怪的是，奧義書作為集會或聚合的意義，據我所知，從未被遇到過。無論何時出現該詞，它都具有教義、秘密教義的意義，或簡單地被用作構成《智慧部》（jñana-kanda）的哲學論著的標題——智慧部與《行為部》（karma-kanda），亦即吠陀的工作或儀式部分，相對而立。

印度本土哲學家似乎從未想到從 sad（坐下）這個根詞推導出奧義書（upanishad）。他們要麼從 sad 這個根詞推導，意為破壞，假定這些古老的論著之所以得名，是因為它們旨在藉由神聖啟示摧毀激情與無明；要麼從 sad 這個根詞推導，意為接近，因為梵天的知識藉由奧義書而接近我們，或者因為我們藉助奧義書而接近梵天。商羯羅在其《泰蒂利亞奧義書》第二卷第九節的注疏中提出的另一種解釋，則是最高的福樂包含於奧義書中（para// sreyo 'sya //z nisha////am）。
這些解釋看似故意曲解，以至於難以理解本地學者意見的一致性。然而，我們應當考量到一個廣泛存在於半受教育者之間的傾向，即對任何能夠解釋一個詞最普遍含義的詞源學都不加批判地接受。《阿蘭耶卡》（Araṇyakas）中充滿了
2 M. M.，《古代梵文文學史》，第318頁；Colebrooke，《論文集》，第一冊，第92頁；Regnaud，《資料匯編》，第7頁。
引言。XXXI 這類詞源學論述，大概從未被視為現代意義上真正的詞源學，而不過是文字遊戲，用以某種方式說明其含義。奧義書固然旨在摧毀無知與情慾，是以再也沒有比其詞源意義應為「摧毀者」更為自然的事了。
梵文語言的歷史與特質幾乎毫無疑問地表明，奧義書（upanishad）最初的意思是聚會，特別是指由學生組成的聚會，他們恭敬地圍坐在師長周圍。
單獨使用 upa 時，sad 早在《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌中就出現，意為恭敬地接近：—
《梨俱吠陀》第九卷第二讚歌第6頌。Namasa it upa sidata，「以讚美之心親近他」。另參《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第一三讚歌第11頌；第一卷第六五讚歌第1頌。
